subject: HSC Mathematics Extension 1
description: Deep detail for each syllabus dot-point, supporting error analysis, Socratic questioning, worked examples and marking feedback skills.

ME-F1.1.name: Graphical relationships — reciprocal and square
ME-F1.1.summary: Sketch y = 1/f(x) and y = [f(x)]² from a given graph of y = f(x) using key feature analysis.
keyConcepts: For y = 1/f(x): zeros of f(x) become vertical asymptotes; asymptotes of f(x) become zeros; where f(x) > 0, so is 1/f(x); where f(x) is large, 1/f(x) is small
For y = [f(x)]²: always ≥ 0; zeros of f(x) remain zeros; turning points of f(x) give turning points; negative y-values are reflected above x-axis
Sign analysis: the sign of f(x) determines the sign of 1/f(x)
Gradient relationship: if f(x) is increasing, 1/f(x) is decreasing (for positive f)
Asymptote rule: vertical asymptotes of 1/f(x) occur where f(x) = 0
commonErrors: Forgetting that zeros of f become asymptotes in 1/f(x) — not zeros
Drawing 1/f(x) below the x-axis when f(x) is positive — it should stay positive
Incorrectly reflecting [f(x)]² — negative portions of f(x) go above x-axis, not below
Missing the turning points that arise in [f(x)]² at the zeros of f(x)
Confusing the behaviour near asymptotes of f(x) in the reciprocal graph
examTips: Always mark zeros, asymptotes and turning points of f(x) first, then transform each
For 1/f(x): ask yourself — where is f(x) = 0? Those become your asymptotes
For [f(x)]²: the graph never goes below the x-axis
Check the sign of the reciprocal carefully — it always matches f(x)
socraticPrompts: If f(3) = 0, what happens to 1/f(x) at x = 3? Why?
If f(x) = −2 at some point, what does [f(x)]² equal there? Where does that point go?
As x approaches a zero of f(x), what happens to 1/f(x)? Draw what you'd expect.
If f(x) is very large and positive, is 1/f(x) large or small? Positive or negative?

steps: Identify zeros, asymptotes and turning points of f(x)
Apply transformation rules to each key feature
Determine sign in each interval
Sketch the transformed graph with correct shape

ME-F1.2.name: Inequalities involving functions
ME-F1.2.summary: Solve inequalities graphically and algebraically using sign diagrams and critical values.
keyConcepts: Sign diagram: a number line showing where an expression is positive, negative or zero
Critical values: x-values where the expression equals zero or is undefined
For |f(x)| > a: solve f(x) > a OR f(x) < −a
For |f(x)| < a: solve −a < f(x) < a
For rational inequalities: never multiply both sides by an expression that could be negative; instead bring everything to one side and use a sign diagram
Graphical method: sketch both sides and find where one is above the other
commonErrors: Multiplying both sides of an inequality by a variable expression (which might be negative) without considering sign change
Forgetting to flip the inequality sign when multiplying/dividing by a negative number
Missing solutions in absolute value inequalities — forgetting the OR case
Incorrectly stating the solution set — use correct interval notation or set notation
Not checking critical values — x-values that make denominator zero must be excluded
examTips: For fraction inequalities: bring all terms to one side, combine over common denominator, then use sign diagram
Always test a value in each interval of the sign diagram to confirm the sign
For absolute value: split into two cases — always write both cases explicitly
State the final answer as an interval or union of intervals, not just critical values
socraticPrompts: If I multiply both sides of x > 2 by −1, what happens to the inequality? Why?
How would you solve (x−1)/(x+2) > 0? Why can't you just multiply both sides by (x+2)?
What does |x − 3| < 2 mean geometrically on a number line?
Can you sketch y = (x−1)/(x+2) and y = 0 to find where the fraction is positive?

steps: Rearrange so one side is zero
Find all critical values (zeros and undefined points)
Draw sign diagram
Read off solution intervals
State answer in correct notation

ME-F1.3.name: Inverse functions
ME-F1.3.summary: Find and graph inverse functions; apply domain restrictions to ensure the inverse is a function.
keyConcepts: f⁻¹(x) is the inverse of f(x): f(f⁻¹(x)) = x and f⁻¹(f(x)) = x
To find f⁻¹(x): write y = f(x), swap x and y, solve for y
Graphical relationship: f⁻¹ is the reflection of f in the line y = x
Horizontal line test: f has an inverse function iff every horizontal line meets the graph at most once (i.e. f is one-to-one)
Domain of f⁻¹ = Range of f; Range of f⁻¹ = Domain of f
If f is not one-to-one, restrict the domain of f to make it so, then find the inverse
commonErrors: Confusing f⁻¹(x) with 1/f(x) — the inverse function is NOT the reciprocal
Not swapping domain and range when stating the inverse function's domain and range
Forgetting to apply domain restrictions when f is not one-to-one (e.g. f(x) = x²)
Graphing f⁻¹ without reflecting correctly — every point (a,b) on f becomes (b,a) on f⁻¹
Incorrect algebra when solving for y after swapping x and y
examTips: Always state the domain of f⁻¹ — it equals the range of f
Check your answer: compute f(f⁻¹(x)) — it should equal x
Draw y = x as a dashed guideline when sketching inverse functions
For f(x) = x²: restrict domain to x ≥ 0 to get f⁻¹(x) = √x
socraticPrompts: If f(2) = 7, what does f⁻¹(7) equal? Why?
Why does f(x) = x² not have an inverse over all real x? What goes wrong?
If I reflect y = 2x + 1 in the line y = x, what equation do I get?
What is the domain of f⁻¹ if f has domain [−3, 5] and range [0, 8]?

steps: Write y = f(x)
Swap x and y to get x = f(y)
Solve for y to get f⁻¹(x)
State domain and range of f⁻¹
Sketch both f and f⁻¹ with the line y = x

ME-F1.4.name: Parametric representation of curves
ME-F1.4.summary: Use parametric equations to represent and sketch curves; convert between parametric and Cartesian form.
keyConcepts: Parametric form: x = f(t), y = g(t) where t is the parameter
To convert to Cartesian: eliminate t by solving one equation for t and substituting into the other
Parametric parabola: x = 2at, y = at² represents x² = 4ay with vertex at origin
Parametric circle: x = r cosθ, y = r sinθ represents x² + y² = r²
The parameter t often represents time — points are traced in a specific direction as t increases
Domain restrictions on t may limit which part of the Cartesian curve is traced
commonErrors: Eliminating t incorrectly — squaring both sides without checking for extraneous solutions
Ignoring domain restrictions from the parameter when stating the Cartesian equation
Confusing the standard parametric form for a parabola: x = 2at, y = at² (not x = at², y = 2at)
Not recognising that different parameter ranges trace different portions of the same Cartesian curve
Mixing up which trigonometric identity to use when eliminating θ from circular parametric equations
examTips: For parabola: remember x = 2at, y = at² gives x² = 4ay; or use x = at², y = 2at for x² = 4ay as well — check which form is given
For circle: use cos²θ + sin²θ = 1 to eliminate θ
Always state the Cartesian equation AND any restrictions on x or y that come from the parameter range
If t ∈ [0, 2π] for a circle, you get the full circle; if t ∈ [0, π], only the upper semicircle
socraticPrompts: If x = 2t and y = t², can you find y in terms of x? What curve is this?
For x = 3cosθ, y = 3sinθ, what is x² + y²? What shape does this describe?
What happens to the point (x, y) as t increases from 0 to 1 in x = t, y = t²?
If t ≥ 0 only, does that change which part of the curve you draw?

steps: Write the parametric equations
Solve one equation for the parameter t
Substitute into the other equation
Simplify to get Cartesian form
State any domain/range restrictions

ME-F2.1.name: Remainder and factor theorems
ME-F2.1.summary: Use remainder and factor theorems to analyse and factorise polynomials.
keyConcepts: Remainder theorem: when P(x) is divided by (x − a), the remainder is P(a)
Factor theorem: (x − a) is a factor of P(x) if and only if P(a) = 0
Polynomial long division: divide P(x) by D(x) to get P(x) = D(x)·Q(x) + R(x)
Degree of remainder R(x) is always less than the degree of D(x)
To find all factors: use factor theorem to find one root, then divide to reduce degree, repeat
commonErrors: Substituting the wrong value — for factor (x − a), substitute x = a not x = −a
Arithmetic errors during polynomial long division — missing terms or sign errors
Forgetting that the remainder theorem gives the REMAINDER, not the quotient
Not checking all rational roots systematically — use the rational root theorem to guide guessing
Leaving the polynomial partially factorised when a full factorisation is possible
examTips: Try small integer values (±1, ±2, ±3) first when looking for rational roots
After finding one factor by trial, always do polynomial division to reduce the degree
Check your factorisation by expanding — the result should equal the original polynomial
Remember: if P(a) = 0, then (x − a) is a factor — write this conclusion explicitly in exams
socraticPrompts: If P(2) = 0, what does the factor theorem tell you about P(x)?
How would you find the remainder when x³ − 3x + 1 is divided by (x − 2)?
After finding that (x − 1) is a factor, how do you find the remaining factors?
Why does polynomial long division always give a remainder of lower degree than the divisor?

steps: Find a root by trial using the factor theorem
Perform polynomial long division by the corresponding factor
Factorise the quotient further if possible
Write the complete factorisation
Solve P(x) = 0 if required

ME-F2.2.name: Sums and products of roots
ME-F2.2.summary: Use Vieta's formulas to relate roots and coefficients of polynomials without solving.
keyConcepts: For ax² + bx + c = 0 with roots α, β: α + β = −b/a, αβ = c/a
For ax³ + bx² + cx + d = 0 with roots α, β, γ: α+β+γ = −b/a, αβ+βγ+γα = c/a, αβγ = −d/a
For ax⁴ + bx³ + cx² + dx + e = 0 with roots α,β,γ,δ: sum = −b/a, sum of pairs = c/a, sum of triples = −d/a, product = e/a
Symmetric functions of roots can be evaluated using Vieta's formulas without finding the roots
α² + β² = (α + β)² − 2αβ and similar identities are used to find derived expressions
commonErrors: Getting the sign wrong — sum of roots = −b/a NOT +b/a for ax² + bx + c = 0
Dividing by the wrong coefficient — always divide by the leading coefficient a, not 1
Errors in derived expressions — forgetting the factor of 2 in α² + β² = (α+β)² − 2αβ
Using the formulas for a monic polynomial when the polynomial is not monic (leading coefficient ≠ 1)
Not checking whether roots are real before drawing conclusions about their values
examTips: Write out Vieta's formulas explicitly at the start — don't rely on memory under pressure
For α² + β²: always expand (α+β)² = α² + 2αβ + β², then rearrange
For 1/α + 1/β: write as (α+β)/(αβ) — use Vieta's for both numerator and denominator
Check: the formulas apply to ALL roots, including complex ones — but HSC usually involves real roots
socraticPrompts: Without solving x² − 5x + 6 = 0, can you find α + β and αβ? Then find α² + β².
If the roots of x³ + px + q = 0 are α, β, γ, what is α + β + γ? Why?
How would you find 1/α + 1/β using Vieta's formulas? Write it as a single fraction first.
If αβ = 3 and α + β = 5, can you find (α − β)²?

steps: Identify the polynomial and write Vieta's formulas
Calculate the elementary symmetric expressions
Use algebraic identities to find the required expression
Substitute Vieta values to get the numerical answer

ME-T1.1.name: Define and use inverse trigonometric functions
ME-T1.1.summary: Define sin⁻¹, cos⁻¹, tan⁻¹ with restricted domains; evaluate exact values and sketch their graphs.
keyConcepts: sin⁻¹x: domain [−1, 1], range [−π/2, π/2]
cos⁻¹x: domain [−1, 1], range [0, π]
tan⁻¹x: domain all reals ℝ, range (−π/2, π/2)
Exact values: sin⁻¹(1/2) = π/6, cos⁻¹(0) = π/2, tan⁻¹(1) = π/4 etc.
The restriction is necessary to make each trig function one-to-one so its inverse exists
sin⁻¹x is an odd function; cos⁻¹x is neither odd nor even; tan⁻¹x is an odd function
Complementary relationship: sin⁻¹x + cos⁻¹x = π/2 for x ∈ [−1, 1]
commonErrors: Confusing sin⁻¹x with (sinx)⁻¹ = 1/sinx — the notation means inverse function, not reciprocal
Giving answers outside the principal range — sin⁻¹(1/2) = π/6, NOT 5π/6
Wrong range for cos⁻¹ — it returns values in [0, π], never negative values
Forgetting that sin⁻¹(−x) = −sin⁻¹(x) but cos⁻¹(−x) = π − cos⁻¹(x)
Evaluating sin(sin⁻¹(2)) — this is undefined since 2 is outside [−1, 1]
examTips: Memorise the three domain-range pairs — they come up in every exam
Use sin⁻¹x + cos⁻¹x = π/2 as a shortcut when both appear in the same problem
For compound expressions like sin(cos⁻¹(3/5)): draw a right triangle
Sketch all three inverse trig graphs — know their shape, intercepts and asymptotes
socraticPrompts: Why can't we define sin⁻¹x over all real x? What goes wrong with sin(x)?
What is sin⁻¹(sin(π)) equal to? Be careful — it's NOT π.
If cos⁻¹x = π/3, what is x? What is sin⁻¹x?
Draw a right triangle to find sin(cos⁻¹(4/5)) without a calculator.

steps: Identify the inverse trig function and its range
Find the exact value using known trig ratios
Check the answer is within the principal range
For compound expressions, use a right triangle or identity

ME-T1.2.name: Trigonometric identities and equations
ME-T1.2.summary: Prove and apply compound angle, double angle formulas and the auxiliary angle method.
keyConcepts: sin(A ± B) = sinA cosB ± cosA sinB
cos(A ± B) = cosA cosB ∓ sinA sinB
tan(A ± B) = (tanA ± tanB) / (1 ∓ tanA tanB)
sin2A = 2sinA cosA
cos2A = cos²A − sin²A = 2cos²A − 1 = 1 − 2sin²A
tan2A = 2tanA / (1 − tan²A)
Auxiliary angle: a sinx + b cosx = R sin(x + α) where R = √(a²+b²), tanα = b/a
commonErrors: Sign errors in cos(A − B) — the minus between terms in expansion becomes a plus
Using the wrong form of cos2A — choose the form that simplifies the problem
Auxiliary angle: wrong formula for α — ensure tanα = b/a not a/b
Not simplifying the auxiliary angle expression back to find the max/min value
Forgetting that R must be positive in the auxiliary angle method
examTips: Learn all compound angle formulas — they are NOT given in the HSC formula sheet for Ext1
For double angle proofs: usually start from one side and work to the other — never work both sides at once
Auxiliary angle: once R sin(x + α) is found, the maximum value is R and minimum is −R
In exams, show the derivation of R and α explicitly — don't just state the result
socraticPrompts: Can you expand sin(45° + 30°) using the compound angle formula and check it equals sin75°?
If sin2A = 2sinA cosA, can you derive a formula for sin3A? Hint: sin3A = sin(2A + A).
What is the maximum value of 3sinx + 4cosx? How do you find it?
Why are there three forms of cos2A? Which one would you choose if you needed to integrate cos²x?

steps: State the relevant identity/formula
Substitute and expand carefully
Simplify using Pythagorean or other identities
State the final result clearly

ME-T2.1.name: Trigonometric equations and general solutions
ME-T2.1.summary: Solve trig equations using t = tan(θ/2) substitution and find general solutions.
keyConcepts: General solution: sinx = a → x = sin⁻¹(a) + 2nπ or x = π − sin⁻¹(a) + 2nπ
General solution: cosx = a → x = ±cos⁻¹(a) + 2nπ
General solution: tanx = a → x = tan⁻¹(a) + nπ
t-formula: let t = tan(θ/2), then sinθ = 2t/(1+t²), cosθ = (1−t²)/(1+t²), tanθ = 2t/(1−t²)
The t-substitution converts trig equations into algebraic (polynomial) equations
Note: t-substitution excludes θ = π (since tan(π/2) is undefined) — check this solution separately
commonErrors: Forgetting the n in the general solution — the answer is a family of solutions, not a single value
Losing the solution θ = π when using the t-substitution — always check separately
Wrong period in general solution: tan has period π, but sin and cos have period 2π
Sign errors in the t-formula substitutions
Not simplifying the algebraic equation fully before solving
examTips: Write the general solution with 'n ∈ ℤ' explicitly — examiners look for this
After t-substitution, solve the algebraic equation and convert t back to θ
Always check θ = π separately when using t = tan(θ/2)
If the domain is restricted (e.g. 0 ≤ θ ≤ 2π), list all specific solutions from the general formula
socraticPrompts: What are ALL the solutions to sinx = 1/2? Not just the ones in [0, 2π].
If t = tan(θ/2) = 1, what is θ? And what does sinθ equal using the t-formula?
Why does using t = tan(θ/2) miss the solution θ = π? What goes wrong?
How is the general solution for tanx = 1 different from the general solution for sinx = 1/√2?

steps: Substitute t = tan(θ/2) and rewrite equation algebraically
Solve the polynomial equation for t
Convert back: θ = 2tan⁻¹(t)
Check θ = π separately
State all solutions (general or within given domain)

ME-C1.1.name: Rates of change and their applications
ME-C1.1.summary: Interpret derivatives as rates and solve related rates problems using the chain rule.
keyConcepts: The derivative dy/dx represents the instantaneous rate of change of y with respect to x
Related rates: use chain rule — if A depends on r which depends on t, then dA/dt = dA/dr × dr/dt
Set up: identify what is changing, what is constant, and which rates are given/sought
Common related rates: area, volume, distance, angle — all changing with time
Differential equation: an equation relating a function to its derivative, e.g. dy/dt = ky
commonErrors: Differentiating with respect to the wrong variable — always check what you are differentiating with respect to
Forgetting to apply the chain rule when the variable inside a function is itself changing
Not drawing a diagram — related rates problems almost always require a clear diagram
Substituting numerical values before differentiating — differentiate first, then substitute
Sign errors — distinguish between rates that are increasing (positive) and decreasing (negative)
examTips: Always define all variables clearly at the start: 'let r = radius in cm, t = time in seconds'
Write out the chain rule explicitly: dV/dt = dV/dr × dr/dt
Draw a diagram for every related rates problem
After finding the rate, check the sign and units make sense in context
socraticPrompts: If V = (4/3)πr³, what is dV/dr? Now if r is changing with time, what is dV/dt?
A ladder is sliding down a wall. What quantities are changing? What stays the same?
If the radius of a circle is increasing at 2 cm/s, how fast is the area increasing when r = 5?
What does it mean for dV/dt to be negative in a context where water is draining from a tank?

steps: Define all variables with units
Write an equation relating the variables
Differentiate both sides with respect to time
Substitute given values
State the answer with correct units and sign

ME-C1.2.name: Exponential growth and decay
ME-C1.2.summary: Model and solve exponential growth and decay problems using dN/dt = kN.
keyConcepts: The differential equation dN/dt = kN has solution N = N₀eᵏᵗ
k > 0: exponential growth (population, compound interest)
k < 0: exponential decay (radioactive decay, cooling)
Half-life T₁/₂: time for quantity to halve; T₁/₂ = ln2/|k|
Doubling time: time for quantity to double; T_double = ln2/k
Newton's law of cooling: dT/dt = −k(T − T_room) has solution T = T_room + Ae^(−kt)
commonErrors: Using the wrong sign for k — decay has k < 0, which means the exponent is negative
Forgetting to include the initial condition N₀ when writing the general solution
Confusing half-life with the decay constant — they are related by T₁/₂ = ln2/|k|, not T₁/₂ = 1/k
Not converting to the same time units throughout the problem
Rounding k too early — keep exact values until the final answer
examTips: Always verify the solution satisfies the differential equation by differentiating
Use exact values (ln) until the very last step
For Newton's cooling: the temperature approaches T_room asymptotically, never reaches it
State the model clearly: 'N = N₀eᵏᵗ where N₀ = ... and k = ...'
socraticPrompts: If dN/dt = 0.03N and N(0) = 500, what is N(10)?
A substance has half-life of 8 years. What is the decay constant k?
Why does the solution to dN/dt = kN always involve eᵏᵗ? Can you verify by differentiating?
If a population doubles every 12 years, what is k? How long to triple?

steps: Write the differential equation
State the general solution N = N₀eᵏᵗ
Apply initial conditions to find N₀
Apply a second condition to find k
Answer the question and interpret the result

ME-C2.1.name: Differential calculus — further techniques
ME-C2.1.summary: Differentiate inverse trig functions and functions defined parametrically.
keyConcepts: d/dx[sin⁻¹(x/a)] = 1/√(a²−x²)
d/dx[cos⁻¹(x/a)] = −1/√(a²−x²)
d/dx[tan⁻¹(x/a)] = a/(a²+x²)
Parametric differentiation: dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt)
Second derivative parametrically: d²y/dx² = (d/dt[dy/dx])/(dx/dt)
Chain rule applies when differentiating composite inverse trig functions
commonErrors: Forgetting the negative sign in d/dx[cos⁻¹x] = −1/√(1−x²)
Not applying the chain rule when the argument is a function of x, not just x
Dividing dy/dt by dx/dt in the wrong order for parametric differentiation
Using a = 1 in the formula when a ≠ 1 — always check the value of a
Errors computing d²y/dx² parametrically — forgetting to divide by dx/dt again
examTips: Write out the formula with the correct a before differentiating
For parametric: dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt) — write this ratio explicitly
Chain rule reminder: d/dx[sin⁻¹(f(x))] = f'(x)/√(1−[f(x)]²)
Always simplify the result — examiners check simplified form
socraticPrompts: What is d/dx[sin⁻¹(x/3)]? Write out the formula with a = 3 first.
For x = t², y = t³, find dy/dx in terms of t. What is it when t = 2?
Why does d/dx[cos⁻¹x] have a negative sign but d/dx[sin⁻¹x] does not?
If y = tan⁻¹(2x), find dy/dx. Don't forget the chain rule.

steps: Identify the function type (inverse trig or parametric)
Write the appropriate differentiation formula
Apply chain rule if needed
Substitute and simplify
Evaluate at specific point if required

ME-C2.2.name: Integral calculus — further techniques
ME-C2.2.summary: Integrate using inverse trig results and substitution.
keyConcepts: ∫ 1/√(a²−x²) dx = sin⁻¹(x/a) + C
∫ 1/(a²+x²) dx = (1/a)tan⁻¹(x/a) + C
Integration by substitution: let u = g(x), then ∫f(g(x))g'(x)dx = ∫f(u)du
Always change limits of integration when substituting in definite integrals
Recognise when a substitution will simplify the integral — look for a function and its derivative
commonErrors: Forgetting the 1/a factor in ∫1/(a²+x²)dx — it is (1/a)tan⁻¹(x/a), not tan⁻¹(x/a)
Not changing the limits when using substitution in a definite integral
Choosing a substitution that doesn't simplify the integral
Forgetting the + C in indefinite integrals
Not differentiating u to find du correctly before substituting
examTips: Write a = √(something) clearly — identify a before applying the formula
For substitution: write du = ... dx, then express dx in terms of du
Check: differentiate your answer to verify it gives the integrand
For definite integrals with substitution: change limits, don't convert back to x
socraticPrompts: What is ∫1/(9+x²)dx? Identify a first.
For ∫1/√(4−x²)dx, what is a? What is the antiderivative?
To evaluate ∫x·√(x²+1)dx, what substitution would you choose? Why?
After letting u = x², what is du? How does the integral change?

steps: Identify the form: inverse trig standard or requires substitution
State the formula or substitution clearly
Rewrite the integral in the new form
Integrate
Substitute back and add C (or evaluate limits)

ME-C2.3.name: Volumes of solids of revolution
ME-C2.3.summary: Calculate volumes formed by rotating regions about coordinate axes.
keyConcepts: Volume about x-axis: V = π∫[a to b] [f(x)]² dx
Volume about y-axis: V = π∫[c to d] [g(y)]² dy where x = g(y)
Washer method (region between two curves): V = π∫[a to b] ([f(x)]² − [g(x)]²) dx
The disc method considers thin circular discs of thickness δx
Always sketch the region and identify the axis of rotation before integrating
commonErrors: Forgetting the π factor — V = π∫y²dx, not ∫y²dx
Squaring the wrong function — it is [f(x)]², not f(x²)
Incorrect limits of integration — find intersection points carefully
Using x = g(y) incorrectly when rotating about the y-axis — rearrange the function first
Subtracting in the wrong order for the washer method — outer radius² minus inner radius²
examTips: Always draw a diagram — label the axis of rotation and the region clearly
For washer method: V = π∫(R² − r²)dx where R = outer radius, r = inner radius
Check: the volume should be positive — if negative, check your limits or function order
State the integral setup before evaluating — examiners award marks for the setup
socraticPrompts: If y = x² is rotated about the x-axis from x = 0 to x = 2, what is each disc shaped like?
Why do we square f(x) in the volume formula? Where does that come from geometrically?
For the region between y = x and y = x², which function gives the outer radius?
If rotating about the y-axis instead, how does the integral change?

steps: Sketch the region and identify axis of rotation
Write the volume formula with correct radii
Find the limits of integration (intersection points)
Evaluate the integral
State the answer with units (if applicable)

ME-C3.1.name: Differential equations
ME-C3.1.summary: Solve first-order separable differential equations; interpret solutions and slope fields.
keyConcepts: Separable ODE: dy/dx = f(x)g(y) — separate variables to get dy/g(y) = f(x)dx
Integrate both sides: ∫1/g(y) dy = ∫f(x) dx
General solution contains an arbitrary constant C
Particular solution: apply initial condition y(x₀) = y₀ to find C
Slope field: a diagram showing the gradient at each point — solutions are curves that follow the arrows
Verify a solution by differentiating and checking it satisfies the ODE
commonErrors: Incorrectly separating variables — all y terms (including dy) must go to one side
Forgetting the constant of integration on BOTH sides — consolidate into one C
Not applying the initial condition to find the particular solution
Errors with absolute values when integrating 1/y: ∫1/y dy = ln|y| + C
Not considering whether y = 0 is also a solution (equilibrium solution)
examTips: Separate variables clearly — write dy/g(y) = f(x)dx before integrating
Consolidate both constants of integration into a single constant immediately
Check your solution by differentiating — substitute back into the ODE
For slope fields: at each point (x,y), draw a short line with slope = f(x,y)
socraticPrompts: How would you separate variables in dy/dx = xy? Write it with all y's on the left.
After integrating, you get ln|y| = x²/2 + C. How do you solve for y?
If y(0) = 3, how do you use this to find the specific value of C?
What does a slope field tell you about solutions without actually solving the ODE?

steps: Separate variables: move all y terms to left, all x terms to right
Integrate both sides
Solve explicitly for y if possible
Apply initial condition to find C
State the particular solution and verify by substitution

ME-A1.1.name: Permutations
ME-A1.1.summary: Count ordered arrangements using factorial notation and the permutation formula.
keyConcepts: Fundamental counting principle: if task A can be done in m ways and task B in n ways, both together can be done in m × n ways
ⁿPr = n!/(n−r)! — number of ways to arrange r objects from n distinct objects
Arrangements in a line: n distinct objects → n! arrangements
Circular arrangements: (n−1)! arrangements (one object fixed to remove rotational symmetry)
Restrictions: use casework or fix restricted objects first
commonErrors: Forgetting that order matters in permutations — AB and BA are different arrangements
Circular permutations: using n! instead of (n−1)!
Overcounting when identical objects are present — must divide by the factorials of repeated items
Not considering all cases when there are restrictions
Confusing ⁿPr with ⁿCr — permutations are ordered, combinations are not
examTips: For restrictions: place the restricted items first, then fill remaining positions
For circular: fix one person/object and arrange the rest — (n−1)!
Draw slots (__ × __ × ...) and fill them systematically
Always check: does order matter? If yes, use permutations. If no, use combinations.
socraticPrompts: In how many ways can 5 people sit in a row? What if 2 specific people must sit together?
How many 3-letter words can be made from {A, B, C, D, E} without repeating letters?
For circular arrangements of 6 people, why do we use 5! instead of 6!?
If we have 4 red and 3 blue identical beads in a line, how do we avoid overcounting?

steps: Identify whether order matters
Identify any restrictions
Apply the multiplication principle or permutation formula
Handle restrictions using casework or fixing objects
Calculate the final answer

ME-A1.2.name: Combinations
ME-A1.2.summary: Count unordered selections using the combination formula and Pascal's triangle.
keyConcepts: ⁿCr = n!/[r!(n−r)!] — number of ways to choose r objects from n without regard to order
Also written C(n,r) or (n choose r) — the binomial coefficient
Symmetry: ⁿCr = ⁿC(n−r) — choosing r to include = choosing (n−r) to exclude
Pascal's triangle: ⁿCr = ⁿ⁻¹C(r−1) + ⁿ⁻¹Cr
ⁿC0 = ⁿCn = 1; ⁿC1 = n
Selecting with restrictions: use casework — 'at least one' = total − none
commonErrors: Using permutations when order doesn't matter — selecting a committee is a combination
Forgetting the symmetry property — ¹⁰C7 = ¹⁰C3, which is easier to calculate
Errors in 'at least' problems — forgetting to subtract properly
Including or excluding the restriction group incorrectly in casework
Arithmetic errors with large factorials — simplify before calculating
examTips: Always simplify ⁿCr before calculating: cancel common factors in the factorial
For 'at least one': P(at least 1) = Total − P(none)
Draw a list of cases clearly when using casework — don't skip any case
Check: ⁿC0 + ⁿC1 + ... + ⁿCn = 2ⁿ — use this to verify your working
socraticPrompts: From 10 students, how many ways can a group of 3 be chosen? Does the order of selection matter?
Why is ¹⁰C7 equal to ¹⁰C3? What does that mean intuitively?
A committee of 4 must include at least 1 woman from 5 women and 6 men. How many ways?
Can you write out Pascal's triangle to row 5 and find ⁵C2 from it?

steps: Identify this is a selection (unordered) problem
Identify restrictions and set up cases
Apply ⁿCr formula to each case
Add or subtract cases as appropriate
Verify total is sensible

ME-A1.3.name: Binomial theorem and binomial expansion
ME-A1.3.summary: Expand (a + b)ⁿ using the binomial theorem; find general and specific terms.
keyConcepts: Binomial theorem: (a+b)ⁿ = Σ(r=0 to n) ⁿCr × aⁿ⁻ʳ × bʳ
General term: T(r+1) = ⁿCr × aⁿ⁻ʳ × bʳ (note: r starts at 0)
To find the constant term: set the power of x in T(r+1) equal to 0 and solve for r
To find the coefficient of xᵏ: set the power of x equal to k and solve for r
The expansion has (n+1) terms
Sum of all coefficients: set a = b = 1, giving (1+1)ⁿ = 2ⁿ
commonErrors: Off-by-one error: T(r+1) has r starting from 0, so T₁ corresponds to r = 0
Forgetting to raise both a and b to their respective powers in the general term
Sign errors when b is negative: (a − b)ⁿ has b = −b, so signs alternate
Not simplifying ⁿCr before calculating large values
Finding the wrong term — confusing 'the 4th term' with T(r+1) where r+1 = 4, so r = 3
examTips: Always write T(r+1) = ⁿCr × aⁿ⁻ʳ × bʳ as your starting point
For the constant term: write out the power of x and set it to zero
For (1+x)ⁿ: the coefficient of xʳ is simply ⁿCr
Check: the sum of all ⁿCr values (coefficients when a=b=1) equals 2ⁿ
socraticPrompts: What is the general term in the expansion of (x + 2)⁵? Write it using T(r+1).
In (x + 1/x)⁶, for what value of r does the constant term occur?
What is the coefficient of x³ in (2 + x)⁷?
Why does (1+x)ⁿ expanded give coefficients that match Pascal's triangle?

steps: Identify a, b and n
Write the general term T(r+1) = ⁿCr × aⁿ⁻ʳ × bʳ
Set up the equation for the required power of x
Solve for r
Substitute r to find the specific term or coefficient

ME-V1.1.name: Vector concepts and representations
ME-V1.1.summary: Represent, add and scale vectors in component form; find magnitude and unit vectors.
keyConcepts: A vector has both magnitude and direction; a scalar has magnitude only
Component form: v = (x, y) or v = xi + yj
Magnitude: |v| = √(x² + y²)
Unit vector: v̂ = v/|v| — a vector of length 1 in the direction of v
Addition: (a, b) + (c, d) = (a+c, b+d) — add components
Scalar multiplication: k(a, b) = (ka, kb)
Zero vector: (0, 0); equal vectors: same magnitude and direction
commonErrors: Confusing magnitude |v| with the vector v itself
Adding magnitudes instead of components: |a + b| ≠ |a| + |b| in general
Unit vector error: dividing by the wrong magnitude
Not expressing the answer as a vector when a vector is required
Confusing i and j components — i is horizontal (x), j is vertical (y)
examTips: Always state whether your answer is a vector or a scalar
Unit vector: find |v| first, then divide each component by |v|
Check: |v̂| should equal 1 — verify your unit vector
For position vectors: point P at (3, 4) has position vector OP = 3i + 4j
socraticPrompts: If v = 3i + 4j, what is |v|? What is the unit vector in the direction of v?
Can you add vectors (2, 3) and (−1, 5) geometrically? What about algebraically?
Is it true that |a + b| = |a| + |b|? Try a = (1,0) and b = (0,1) to check.
What does it mean for two vectors to be equal if they are at different positions?

steps: Write vectors in component form
Perform the required operation (add, scale, etc.)
Calculate magnitude using Pythagoras
Find unit vector by dividing by magnitude
Interpret geometrically if required

ME-V1.2.name: Dot product and projections
ME-V1.2.summary: Calculate and apply the dot product to find angles, test perpendicularity and compute projections.
keyConcepts: Dot product: a·b = x₁x₂ + y₁y₂ = |a||b|cosθ
Perpendicular vectors: a·b = 0 (and a ≠ 0, b ≠ 0)
Parallel vectors: a = kb for some scalar k
Angle between vectors: cosθ = (a·b)/(|a||b|), where 0 ≤ θ ≤ π
Scalar resolute of b onto a: (a·b)/|a|
Vector resolute of b onto a: [(a·b)/|a|²] × a
commonErrors: Computing a·b as a vector instead of a scalar — the dot product is always a scalar
Using the wrong formula for the angle: forgetting to divide by |a||b|
Concluding vectors are parallel when their dot product is non-zero — parallelism uses a = kb
Projecting b onto a using b's magnitude instead of a's magnitude
Not verifying that θ is between 0 and π when using the dot product angle formula
examTips: Show the formula, then substitute — write a·b = x₁x₂ + y₁y₂ explicitly
Perpendicular check: compute a·b and confirm it equals 0
For projection: the scalar resolute is a number; the vector resolute is a vector — state which is required
cosθ < 0 means the angle is obtuse — this is valid, don't force an acute angle
socraticPrompts: Find the angle between a = (1, 2) and b = (3, −1). Start with a·b.
If a·b = 0, what does that tell you about a and b geometrically?
What is the scalar resolute of b = (4, 3) in the direction of a = (1, 0)?
Why is the dot product a scalar, not a vector? What does it measure geometrically?

steps: Calculate a·b using components
Calculate |a| and |b|
Find the angle or resolute using the appropriate formula
Interpret the result geometrically
State whether the answer is scalar or vector

ME-V1.3.name: Vectors and geometry
ME-V1.3.summary: Use vectors to prove geometric results and find equations of lines.
keyConcepts: Position vector of P: OP⃗ = p
Vector from A to B: AB⃗ = b − a (position vector of B minus position vector of A)
Midpoint M of AB: OM⃗ = (a + b)/2
Three points A, B, C are collinear if AC⃗ = k × AB⃗ for some scalar k
Vector equation of a line: r = a + λd where a is a point on the line and d is the direction vector
Two lines are parallel if their direction vectors are scalar multiples; they intersect if they have a common point
commonErrors: AB⃗ = a − b wrong — it should be b − a (destination minus origin)
Midpoint formula: using a − b instead of (a + b)/2
Collinearity: showing vectors are parallel is necessary but not sufficient — they must share a common point
Line equation: confusing the position vector a with the direction vector d
Not checking if lines actually intersect after assuming they do
examTips: For geometric proofs: express all vectors in terms of a few base vectors
Collinearity: show that one vector is a scalar multiple of another AND they share a point
Midpoint: OM = (a + b)/2 — this is a vector average
For line intersection: set equal and solve for both parameters simultaneously
socraticPrompts: If A has position vector a and B has position vector b, what is AB⃗? And the midpoint?
How can you show that three points are collinear using vectors?
Write the vector equation of the line through (1,2) in the direction (3,−1).
Two lines have equations r = (1,0) + s(2,1) and r = (3,2) + t(1,−1). Do they intersect?

steps: Express all relevant vectors in terms of position vectors
Use vector operations to find required vectors
Apply collinearity or intersection conditions
State conclusion clearly with justification

ME-V1.4.name: Projectile motion using vectors
ME-V1.4.summary: Model projectile motion with vector equations; find range, height and trajectory.
keyConcepts: Acceleration: a = (0, −g) where g ≈ 9.8 m/s² or 10 m/s²
Initial velocity: v₀ = V cosα i + V sinα j where V = speed, α = angle of projection
Velocity: v(t) = (V cosα)i + (V sinα − gt)j
Position: r(t) = (V cosα · t)i + (V sinα · t − ½gt²)j
Maximum height: when vertical velocity = 0, i.e. t = V sinα/g
Range: horizontal distance when y = 0 (back at ground level)
Cartesian equation of trajectory: y = x tanα − gx²/(2V²cos²α)
commonErrors: Not resolving initial velocity into horizontal and vertical components correctly
Forgetting that horizontal velocity is constant (no horizontal acceleration)
Finding max height at max range — these are different times
Using degrees instead of considering radians (HSC usually uses degrees — check context)
Sign error: gravity acts downward so vertical acceleration is −g
examTips: Always start by writing horizontal and vertical components separately
Maximum height: set vy = 0 and solve for t, then substitute into y(t)
Range: set y(t) = 0, solve for t > 0, substitute into x(t)
Trajectory equation: eliminate t from x = V cosα · t and y = V sinα · t − ½gt²
socraticPrompts: A ball is projected at 20 m/s at 30° to the horizontal. What are the horizontal and vertical components of velocity?
Why does the horizontal velocity stay constant throughout the motion?
How do you find the time of flight? What condition are you applying?
Can you derive the trajectory equation y in terms of x by eliminating t?

steps: Resolve initial velocity into components
Write position vector r(t)
For max height: set vertical velocity to zero and find t
For range: set y = 0 and solve for t > 0
Substitute to find the required quantity

ME-S1.1.name: Bernoulli trials and binomial probability
ME-S1.1.summary: Recognise Bernoulli trials and calculate binomial probabilities.
keyConcepts: Bernoulli trial: a single experiment with exactly two outcomes (success/failure), fixed probability p of success
Binomial distribution X ~ B(n, p): n independent Bernoulli trials
P(X = r) = ⁿCr × pʳ × (1−p)ⁿ⁻ʳ for r = 0, 1, 2, ..., n
P(X ≥ r) = 1 − P(X ≤ r−1) — use complement for 'at least' problems
The distribution is valid only when: fixed n, independent trials, constant p, two outcomes
commonErrors: Using the binomial formula when trials are not independent — check this condition first
Confusing p (probability of success) with q = 1−p (probability of failure)
Off-by-one in 'at least r' problems — P(X ≥ r) = 1 − P(X ≤ r−1), not 1 − P(X ≤ r)
Forgetting to include ⁿCr — multiplying only pʳ(1−p)ⁿ⁻ʳ
Not verifying the four conditions for a binomial model
examTips: Always verify: are trials independent? Is p constant? Are there exactly two outcomes?
For 'at least 2': use 1 − P(X=0) − P(X=1)
Write P(X = r) = ⁿCr pʳ qⁿ⁻ʳ explicitly before substituting values
CAS calculator: use binomPdf and binomCdf for checking, but show the formula in working
socraticPrompts: A fair coin is tossed 10 times. Why is this a binomial situation? What are n and p?
Find P(X = 3) when X ~ B(10, 0.4). Write out the formula first.
For P(X ≥ 2), why is it easier to use the complement?
If I draw cards without replacement, is this binomial? Why or why not?

steps: Verify binomial conditions
Identify n and p
Write P(X = r) = ⁿCr pʳ (1−p)ⁿ⁻ʳ
Substitute and calculate
For cumulative probabilities, use complement or add individual terms

ME-S1.2.name: Mean and variance of binomial distribution
ME-S1.2.summary: Calculate and interpret E(X) = np and Var(X) = np(1−p) for a binomial distribution.
keyConcepts: Mean (expected value): E(X) = np
Variance: Var(X) = np(1−p) = npq where q = 1−p
Standard deviation: SD(X) = √(npq)
The mean is the long-run average number of successes in n trials
Larger variance means more spread — occurs when p is close to 0.5
These formulas apply ONLY to the binomial distribution — not a general rule
commonErrors: Using Var(X) = np instead of np(1−p) — forgetting to multiply by (1−p)
Confusing variance and standard deviation — SD = √Var, not Var itself
Applying E(X) = np to non-binomial distributions
Incorrect p — ensure p is the probability of success, not failure
Not interpreting E(X) in context — state what the expected value means
examTips: Write down E(X) = np and Var(X) = npq with the actual values substituted
State: 'On average, we expect ... successes in n trials'
Standard deviation is more interpretable than variance — convert for context questions
If asked to compare two distributions, compare both mean AND standard deviation
socraticPrompts: X ~ B(20, 0.3). Find E(X) and SD(X). What do these values mean in context?
If you toss a coin 100 times, how many heads do you expect? How spread out is the result?
Why is the variance largest when p = 0.5? What does that tell us?
Can E(X) be non-integer for a binomial distribution? Is that possible?

steps: Identify n and p from the binomial distribution
Calculate E(X) = np
Calculate Var(X) = np(1−p)
Calculate SD(X) = √(Var)
Interpret the results in context

ME-S1.3.name: Normal approximation to the binomial
ME-S1.3.summary: Approximate binomial probabilities using the normal distribution with continuity correction.
keyConcepts: When n is large and p is not too close to 0 or 1: X ~ B(n,p) ≈ N(np, npq)
Rule of thumb: np ≥ 5 and nq ≥ 5 for the approximation to be valid
Continuity correction: since X is discrete and Z is continuous, P(X = k) ≈ P(k−0.5 < Y < k+0.5)
P(X ≤ k) ≈ P(Y < k + 0.5) and P(X ≥ k) ≈ P(Y > k − 0.5)
Standardise: Z = (Y − μ)/σ where μ = np and σ = √(npq)
commonErrors: Forgetting the continuity correction — this is a very common mark-losing error
Wrong direction of correction: P(X ≤ k) uses k + 0.5, not k − 0.5
Not checking the conditions np ≥ 5 and nq ≥ 5 before applying the approximation
Using the wrong mean or variance — μ = np, σ² = npq (not np or n)
Not standardising correctly — Z = (X − μ)/σ
examTips: Always state: 'Since np = ... ≥ 5 and nq = ... ≥ 5, the normal approximation is valid'
Apply continuity correction before standardising — write P(X ≤ k) = P(Y < k + 0.5)
Show the standardisation step: Z = (Y − np)/√(npq)
Compare with the exact binomial answer if asked — show both
socraticPrompts: X ~ B(100, 0.4). Why can we approximate with a normal distribution here?
What normal distribution approximates B(50, 0.6)? What are μ and σ?
To find P(X ≤ 25) using the normal approximation, what continuity correction do you apply?
Why do we need the continuity correction? What goes wrong without it?

steps: Verify conditions: np ≥ 5 and nq ≥ 5
State the approximating distribution Y ~ N(np, npq)
Apply continuity correction to the probability
Standardise to find Z
Use standard normal tables or calculator to evaluate
